full_context: According to Appendix A of GB/T 10179-2009, the hydraulic natural frequency of a hydraulic vibration generator is calculated by Equation (A.1): f_h = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k_h}{m}}. Where: f_h = hydraulic natural frequency, Hz; k_h = hydraulic spring stiffness, N/m; m = mass of moving system, kg. This formula describes the frequency characteristic of a hydraulic servo system at resonance.

variable_definitions.name: Hydraulic natural frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Resonant frequency of the moving system of hydraulic vibration generator

variable_definitions.name: Hydraulic spring stiffness
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent spring stiffness of hydraulic system

variable_definitions.name: Mass of moving system
variable_definitions.meaning: Mass of moving parts of hydraulic vibration generator

potential_questions: Given hydraulic spring stiffness k_h=5\times10^{6}\,\mathrm{N/m}, mass of moving system m=50\,\mathrm{kg}, find the hydraulic natural frequency f_h.
task_description: 1. Obtain k_h and m. 2. Calculate k_h/m. 3. Calculate \sqrt{k_h/m}. 4. Calculate f_h = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k_h/m}. 5. Output f_h.
section: Appendix A Characteristics of Hydraulic Vibration Generator

full_context: According to Clause 5.1.3 of GB/T 51306-2018, the relationship between natural angular frequency and natural frequency is expressed by Equation (5.1): \omega_n = 2\pi f_n. Where: \omega_n = natural angular frequency, rad/s; f_n = natural frequency, Hz. This is a basic conversion relation in vibration analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Natural angular frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Angular frequency of free vibration of the system

variable_definitions.name: Natural frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of periodic vibrations per unit time

potential_questions: Given natural frequency f_n=25\,\mathrm{Hz} of a vibration system, find its natural angular frequency \omega_n.
task_description: 1. Obtain f_n. 2. Calculate \omega_n = 2\pi \times f_n. 3. Output \omega_n.

full_context: According to Appendix A of JB/T 12661-2016, the safe fatigue strength of an internal combustion engine connecting rod is calculated by Equation (A.1): S_a = \frac{\sigma_{-1}}{K_f}. Where: S_a = safe fatigue strength, MPa; \sigma_{-1} = fatigue limit under reversed cycling, MPa; K_f = fatigue stress concentration factor. This formula is used for preliminary evaluation of connecting rod fatigue strength.

variable_definitions.name: Safe fatigue strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable fatigue strength considering stress concentration

variable_definitions.name: Fatigue limit under reversed cycling
variable_definitions.meaning: Fatigue limit of material under symmetric cyclic stress

variable_definitions.name: Fatigue stress concentration factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Stress concentration factor considering material sensitivity

potential_questions: Given reversed cycling fatigue limit \sigma_{-1}=350\,\mathrm{MPa} and fatigue stress concentration factor K_f=2.2 for connecting rod material 40Cr, find the safe fatigue strength S_a.
task_description: 1. Obtain \sigma_{-1} and K_f. 2. Calculate S_a = \sigma_{-1}/K_f. 3. Output S_a.
section: Appendix A Safe Fatigue Strength Calculation
discipline: Mechanical Engineering / Internal Combustion Engine Engineering

full_context: According to Chapter 4 of GB/T 14229-1993, gear contact fatigue strength is calculated by Equation (4.1): \sigma_H = Z_H Z_E Z_\varepsilon \sqrt{\frac{F_t}{b d_1} \cdot \frac{u+1}{u}}. Where: \sigma_H = contact stress, MPa; Z_H = zone factor; Z_E = elastic coefficient; Z_\varepsilon = contact ratio factor; F_t = tangential force, N; b = face width, mm; d_1 = pitch diameter of pinion, mm; u = gear ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Contact stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum stress at gear tooth contact surface

variable_definitions.name: Zone factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering curvature at pitch point

variable_definitions.name: Elastic coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering elastic modulus of materials

variable_definitions.name: Contact ratio factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering contact ratio

variable_definitions.name: Tangential force
variable_definitions.meaning: Transmitted tangential force on gear

variable_definitions.name: Face width
variable_definitions.meaning: Axial width of gear

variable_definitions.name: Pinion pitch diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference diameter of pinion

variable_definitions.name: Gear ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of number of teeth of gear to pinion

potential_questions: Given gear parameters: Z_H=2.5, Z_E=189.8\,\sqrt{\mathrm{MPa}}, Z_\varepsilon=0.9, F_t=2000\,\mathrm{N}, b=30\,\mathrm{mm}, d_1=60\,\mathrm{mm}, u=3, find contact stress \sigma_H.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters. 2. Calculate F_t/(b d_1). 3. Calculate (u+1)/u. 4. Multiply results of Step 2 and Step 3. 5. Calculate square root of Step 4 result. 6. Calculate \sigma_H = Z_H \times Z_E \times Z_\varepsilon \times \text{result of Step 5}. 7. Output \sigma_H.

full_context: According to Chapter 5 of GB/T 14230-1993, gear bending fatigue strength is calculated by Equation (5.1): \sigma_F = \frac{F_t}{b m_n} \times Y_F \times Y_S \times Y_\beta. Where: \sigma_F = bending stress, MPa; F_t = tangential force, N; b = face width, mm; m_n = normal module, mm; Y_F = tooth form factor; Y_S = stress correction factor; Y_\beta = helix angle factor.

variable_definitions.name: Bending stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Bending stress at gear root

variable_definitions.name: Tangential force
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 4.1

variable_definitions.name: Face width
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 4.1

variable_definitions.name: Normal module
variable_definitions.meaning: Module on normal section of gear

variable_definitions.name: Tooth form factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering tooth profile

variable_definitions.name: Stress correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering stress concentration

variable_definitions.name: Helix angle factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient considering helix angle

potential_questions: Given gear parameters: F_t=1500\,\mathrm{N}, b=25\,\mathrm{mm}, m_n=3\,\mathrm{mm}, Y_F=2.8, Y_S=1.6, Y_\beta=0.9, find bending stress \sigma_F.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters. 2. Calculate F_t/(b m_n). 3. Calculate \sigma_F = \text{result of Step 2} \times Y_F \times Y_S \times Y_\beta. 4. Output \sigma_F.

full_context: According to Appendix B of JB/T 13163-2017, fatigue life of brake components is estimated by Equation (B.1): N_f = \left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_{-1}}\right)^{-m}. Where: N_f = fatigue life (number of cycles); \sigma_a = working stress amplitude, MPa; \sigma_{-1} = material fatigue limit, MPa; m = material constant (slope of S-N curve). This formula is based on the power-law S-N curve.

variable_definitions.name: Fatigue life
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of cycles to fatigue failure under given stress amplitude

variable_definitions.name: Working stress amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual stress amplitude under operating conditions

variable_definitions.name: Material fatigue limit
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation A.1

variable_definitions.name: Material constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Slope of S-N curve in log-log coordinates

potential_questions: Given material fatigue limit \sigma_{-1}=300\,\mathrm{MPa}, material constant m=8, working stress amplitude \sigma_a=200\,\mathrm{MPa}, estimate fatigue life N_f.
task_description: 1. Obtain \sigma_a, \sigma_{-1} and m. 2. Calculate stress ratio \sigma_a/\sigma_{-1}. 3. Calculate N_f = (\sigma_a/\sigma_{-1})^{-m}. 4. Output N_f.

full_context: According to Chapter 3 of GB/T 13475-1992, thermal resistance of building components is calculated by Equation (3.1): R = \frac{\delta}{\lambda}. Where: R = thermal resistance, m²·K/W; \delta = material thickness, m; \lambda = thermal conductivity, W/(m·K). This formula is fundamental for steady-state heat conduction calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Thermal resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Ability of material to resist heat transfer

variable_definitions.name: Material thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Material thickness in heat flow direction

variable_definitions.name: Thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Physical quantity describing thermal conductivity of material

potential_questions: Given insulation thickness \delta=0.1\,\mathrm{m} and thermal conductivity \lambda=0.04\,\mathrm{W/(m·K)}, find thermal resistance R of the material.
task_description: 1. Obtain \delta and \lambda. 2. Calculate R = \delta/\lambda. 3. Output R.

full_context: According to Chapter 4 of GB/T 10295-1988, thermal conductivity of thermal insulation is calculated by Equation (4.1): \lambda = \frac{Q\delta}{A\Delta T}. Where: \lambda = thermal conductivity, W/(m·K); Q = heat flow rate, W; \delta = specimen thickness, m; A = specimen area, m²; \Delta T = temperature difference across specimen, K. This is the basic formula for measuring thermal conductivity by heat flow meter method.

variable_definitions.name: Thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 3.1

variable_definitions.name: Heat flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat passing through specimen per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Specimen thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 3.1

variable_definitions.name: Specimen area
variable_definitions.meaning: Specimen area perpendicular to heat flow direction

variable_definitions.name: Temperature difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Temperature difference between two surfaces of specimen

potential_questions: Given heat flow rate Q=50\,\mathrm{W}, specimen thickness \delta=0.05\,\mathrm{m}, specimen area A=0.25\,\mathrm{m^2}, temperature difference \Delta T=40\,\mathrm{K}, find thermal conductivity \lambda of the material.
task_description: 1. Obtain Q, \delta, A, \Delta T. 2. Calculate Q\times\delta. 3. Calculate A\times\Delta T. 4. Calculate \lambda = (Q\times\delta)/(A\times\Delta T). 5. Output \lambda.

full_context: According to Appendix B of GB/T 31845-2015, heat flow through composite structure of electrical and electronic equipment is calculated by Equation (B.1): Q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}}. Where: Q = heat flow rate, W; \Delta T = temperature difference across structure, K; R_{total} = total thermal resistance, K/W. This formula is based on series thermal resistance principle and applicable to thermal design of multilayer structures.

variable_definitions.name: Heat flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 4.1

variable_definitions.name: Temperature difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 4.1

variable_definitions.name: Total thermal resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total thermal resistance of multilayer structure in series

potential_questions: Given total thermal resistance R_{total}=0.5\,\mathrm{K/W} and internal-external temperature difference \Delta T=60\,\mathrm{K} of electronic equipment cooling structure, find heat flow Q through the structure.
task_description: 1. Obtain \Delta T and R_{total}. 2. Calculate Q = \Delta T/R_{total}. 3. Output Q.
section: Appendix B Composite Structure Thermal Design

formula: p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g h_2
full_context: According to fundamental principles of fluid mechanics, Bernoulli’s equation holds along a streamline for steady flow of ideal incompressible fluid in gravity field: p_1 + \frac12\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1 = p_2 + \frac12\rho v_2^2 + \rho g h_2. Where: p = static pressure, Pa; \rho = density, kg/m³; v = flow velocity, m/s; g = gravitational acceleration, m/s²; h = elevation, m. This equation expresses the principle of mechanical energy conservation.

variable_definitions.name: Static pressure
variable_definitions.meaning: Pressure at sections 1 and 2

variable_definitions.name: Flow velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Flow velocity at sections 1 and 2

variable_definitions.name: Elevation
variable_definitions.meaning: Height of sections 1 and 2 relative to reference plane

variable_definitions.name: Density
variable_definitions.meaning: Fluid density

variable_definitions.name: Gravitational acceleration
variable_definitions.meaning: Acceleration due to Earth’s gravity

potential_questions: Given pipe section 1: p_1=150\,\mathrm{kPa}, v_1=1.5\,\mathrm{m/s}, h_1=2\,\mathrm{m}; section 2: v_2=3\,\mathrm{m/s}, h_2=1\,\mathrm{m}. Find p_2. \rho=1000\,\mathrm{kg/m^3}, g=9.8\,\mathrm{m/s^2}.
task_description: 1. Obtain all parameters. 2. Calculate Bernoulli constant C = p_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1. 3. Calculate p_2 = C - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 - \rho g h_2. 4. Output p_2.

full_context: According to mass conservation principle, continuity equation for steady flow of incompressible fluid in a pipe is expressed as: A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2. Where: A₁, A₂ = cross-sectional areas of pipe, m²; v₁, v₂ = average flow velocities at corresponding sections, m/s. This equation shows that volume flow rate is conserved along flow path.

variable_definitions.name: Cross-sectional area
variable_definitions.meaning: Areas of pipe at sections 1 and 2

variable_definitions.name: Average flow velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Average flow velocities at sections 1 and 2

potential_questions: Given pipe diameter changes from d_1=80\,\mathrm{mm} to d_2=50\,\mathrm{mm}, if flow velocity in larger section v_1=2\,\mathrm{m/s}, find flow velocity v_2 in smaller section.
task_description: 1. Calculate A_1 = \pi (d_1/2)^2. 2. Calculate A_2 = \pi (d_2/2)^2. 3. Calculate v_2 = (A_1/A_2) \times v_1. 4. Output v_2.

full_context: Reynolds number is a dimensionless number for judging flow regime, calculated by: \mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}. Where: \mathrm{Re} = Reynolds number; \rho = fluid density, kg/m³; v = characteristic flow velocity, m/s; D = characteristic length (e.g., pipe diameter), m; \mu = dynamic viscosity of fluid, Pa·s. Flow is laminar if \mathrm{Re}<2000, turbulent if \mathrm{Re}>4000.

variable_definitions.name: Reynolds number
variable_definitions.meaning: Dimensionless ratio of inertial force to viscous force

variable_definitions.name: Density
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 11

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic flow velocity
variable_definitions.meaning: Average flow velocity in pipe

variable_definitions.name: Characteristic length
variable_definitions.meaning: Pipe diameter

variable_definitions.name: Dynamic viscosity
variable_definitions.meaning: Ability of fluid to resist shear deformation

potential_questions: Given pipe diameter D=40\,\mathrm{mm}, water velocity v=1.2\,\mathrm{m/s}, fluid density \rho=998\,\mathrm{kg/m^3}, dynamic viscosity \mu=1.002\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{Pa\cdot s} at 20\,^{\circ}\!\mathrm{C}, calculate Reynolds number and judge flow regime.
task_description: 1. Obtain \rho, v, D, \mu. 2. Calculate \mathrm{Re} = \rho v D / \mu. 3. Judge: laminar if \mathrm{Re}<2000, transition if 2000\le \mathrm{Re}\le 4000, turbulent if \mathrm{Re}>4000. 4. Output \mathrm{Re} and flow regime.

full_context: According to Fourier’s law (basic law of heat conduction), heat flux is proportional to temperature gradient: q = -\lambda \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}t}{\mathrm{d}x}\right). Where: q = heat flux, W/m²; \lambda = thermal conductivity of material, W/(m·K); \frac{\mathrm{d}t}{\mathrm{d}x} = temperature gradient, K/m. The minus sign indicates heat flows opposite to the direction of temperature increase.

variable_definitions.name: Heat flux
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat transfer per unit time per unit area

variable_definitions.name: Thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 3.1

variable_definitions.name: Temperature gradient
variable_definitions.meaning: Rate of temperature change in space

potential_questions: Given thermal conductivity \lambda=0.8\,\mathrm{W/(m·K)} and measured temperature gradient \frac{\mathrm{d}t}{\mathrm{d}x}=-80\,\mathrm{K/m}, find magnitude and direction of heat flux q.
task_description: 1. Obtain \lambda and \frac{\mathrm{d}t}{\mathrm{d}x}. 2. Calculate q = -\lambda \frac{\mathrm{d}t}{\mathrm{d}x}. 3. Note sign: positive q means heat flows in positive x-direction; negative q means negative x-direction. 4. Output magnitude and direction of q.

full_context: For one‑dimensional steady heat conduction through a single plane wall, heat transfer rate is calculated by: Q = \lambda A \frac{t_1 - t_2}{\delta}. Where: Q = heat flow rate, W; A = heat transfer area, m²; t₁, t₂ = temperatures on two sides of wall, K; \delta = wall thickness, m; \lambda = thermal conductivity, W/(m·K). This formula is derived by integrating Fourier’s law.

variable_definitions.name: Heat flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Heat passing through plane wall per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 14

variable_definitions.name: Heat transfer area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area perpendicular to heat flow direction

variable_definitions.name: Temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Surface temperatures on two sides of plane wall

variable_definitions.name: Thickness
variable_definitions.meaning: Thickness of plane wall

potential_questions: Given concrete wall thickness \delta=0.2\,\mathrm{m}, area A=15\,\mathrm{m^2}, thermal conductivity \lambda=1.2\,\mathrm{W/(m·K)}, internal and external surface temperatures t_1=25\,^{\circ}\!\mathrm{C}, t_2=5\,^{\circ}\!\mathrm{C}, find heat flow rate Q through wall.
task_description: 1. Obtain \lambda, A, t_1, t_2, \delta. 2. Calculate temperature difference \Delta t = t_1 - t_2. 3. Calculate Q = \lambda \times A \times \Delta t / \delta. 4. Output Q.

full_context: For radial steady heat conduction through a single cylindrical wall, heat flow rate is calculated by: Q = \frac{2\pi \lambda L (t_1 - t_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}. Where: L = cylinder length, m; r₁, r₂ = inner and outer radii, m; t₁, t₂ = inner and outer surface temperatures, K; \lambda = thermal conductivity, W/(m·K). This formula is used in engineering calculations such as pipe insulation.

variable_definitions.name: Heat flow rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 15

variable_definitions.name: Thermal conductivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 14

variable_definitions.name: Length
variable_definitions.meaning: Axial length of cylinder

variable_definitions.name: Temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 15

variable_definitions.name: Radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Inner and outer radii of cylinder

potential_questions: Given steam pipe inner radius r_1=0.08\,\mathrm{m}, outer radius r_2=0.10\,\mathrm{m}, length L=30\,\mathrm{m}, thermal conductivity \lambda=50\,\mathrm{W/(m·K)}, inner and outer surface temperatures t_1=250\,^{\circ}\!\mathrm{C}, t_2=40\,^{\circ}\!\mathrm{C}, find heat loss Q through pipe wall.
task_description: 1. Obtain \lambda, L, t_1, t_2, r_1, r_2. 2. Calculate temperature difference \Delta t = t_1 - t_2. 3. Calculate \frac{r_2}{r_1}. 4. Calculate \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right). 5. Calculate Q = \frac{2\pi \lambda L \Delta t}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}. 6. Output Q.

full_context: Theoretical stress concentration factor is calculated by: K_t = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}. Where: K_t = theoretical stress concentration factor; \sigma_{max} = maximum stress at notch, MPa; \sigma_{nom} = nominal stress (without considering stress concentration), MPa. This factor is based on elasticity theory and reflects geometric effect on stress distribution.

variable_definitions.name: Theoretical stress concentration factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of maximum stress at notch to nominal stress

variable_definitions.name: Maximum stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum stress at notch or geometric discontinuity

variable_definitions.name: Nominal stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Stress calculated by simple formula based on net section

potential_questions: Given nominal stress at net section of plate with circular hole \sigma_{nom}=100\,\mathrm{MPa} and measured maximum stress at hole edge \sigma_{max}=300\,\mathrm{MPa}, find theoretical stress concentration factor K_t.
task_description: 1. Obtain \sigma_{max} and \sigma_{nom}. 2. Calculate K_t = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{nom}. 3. Output K_t.

full_context: Fatigue strength considering mean stress effect is corrected by Goodman formula: \sigma_a = \sigma_{-1} \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right). Where: \sigma_a = allowable stress amplitude, MPa; \sigma_{-1} = fatigue limit under reversed cycling, MPa; \sigma_m = mean stress, MPa; \sigma_u = ultimate tensile strength of material, MPa. This formula linearizes the effect of mean stress.

variable_definitions.name: Allowable stress amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowable stress amplitude considering mean stress effect

variable_definitions.name: Fatigue limit under reversed cycling
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation A.1

variable_definitions.name: Mean stress
variable_definitions.meaning: Average stress in cyclic loading

variable_definitions.name: Ultimate tensile strength
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum stress material can withstand in tensile test

potential_questions: Given ultimate tensile strength \sigma_u=600\,\mathrm{MPa}, reversed cycling fatigue limit \sigma_{-1}=350\,\mathrm{MPa}, working mean stress \sigma_m=200\,\mathrm{MPa}, find allowable stress amplitude \sigma_a.
task_description: 1. Obtain \sigma_{-1}, \sigma_m, \sigma_u. 2. Calculate \sigma_m/\sigma_u. 3. Calculate 1 - \sigma_m/\sigma_u. 4. Calculate \sigma_a = \sigma_{-1} \times \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_u}\right). 5. Output \sigma_a.

full_context: For damped single‑degree‑of‑freedom system, damped natural angular frequency is calculated by: \omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}. Where: \omega_d = damped natural angular frequency, rad/s; \omega_n = undamped natural angular frequency, rad/s; \zeta = damping ratio, dimensionless. System is underdamped and exhibits decaying oscillation when \zeta<1.

variable_definitions.name: Damped natural angular frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual vibration angular frequency of system considering damping

variable_definitions.name: Undamped natural angular frequency
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 2

variable_definitions.name: Damping ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual damping coefficient to critical damping coefficient

potential_questions: Given undamped natural angular frequency \omega_n=15\,\mathrm{rad/s} and damping ratio \zeta=0.15, find damped natural angular frequency \omega_d.
task_description: 1. Obtain \omega_n and \zeta. 2. Calculate \zeta^2. 3. Calculate 1-\zeta^2. 4. Calculate \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}. 5. Calculate \omega_d = \omega_n \times \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}. 6. Output \omega_d.

full_context: Vibration transmissibility is calculated by: \mathrm{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}. Where: \mathrm{TR} = transmissibility, dimensionless; r = frequency ratio (excitation frequency / natural frequency); \zeta = damping ratio. This formula evaluates vibration isolation effect; vibration is attenuated if \mathrm{TR}<1.

variable_definitions.name: Transmissibility
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of output vibration amplitude to input vibration amplitude

variable_definitions.name: Frequency ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of excitation frequency to undamped natural frequency

variable_definitions.name: Damping ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Same as defined in Equation 18

potential_questions: Given frequency ratio r=3.0 and damping ratio \zeta=0.08 for isolation system, find vibration transmissibility \mathrm{TR} and judge isolation effect (whether \mathrm{TR}<1).
task_description: 1. Obtain r and \zeta. 2. Calculate (2\zeta r)^2. 3. Calculate 1+(2\zeta r)^2. 4. Calculate (1-r^2)^2. 5. Calculate (1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2. 6. Calculate \mathrm{TR} = \sqrt{\frac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}. 7. Judge if \mathrm{TR}<1. 8. Output \mathrm{TR} and judgment.

full_context: For tightened bolt joint under axial variable load, stress amplitude is calculated by: \sigma_a = \frac{C_1 F}{2A}. Where: \sigma_a = stress amplitude, MPa; C_1 = relative stiffness of bolt; F = working tensile force, N; A = critical cross‑section area of bolt, mm². This formula is used for bolt fatigue strength calculation.

variable_definitions.name: Stress amplitude
variable_definitions.meaning: Half of stress variation range in cyclic loading

variable_definitions.name: Relative stiffness
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of bolt stiffness to clamped part stiffness

variable_definitions.name: Working tensile force
variable_definitions.meaning: Amplitude of axial alternating load on bolt

variable_definitions.name: Critical cross‑section area
variable_definitions.meaning: Cross‑section area at minor diameter of bolt thread

potential_questions: Given relative stiffness C_1=0.3, working tensile force F=8000\,\mathrm{N}, minor thread diameter d_1=12\,\mathrm{mm}, find stress amplitude \sigma_a.
task_description: 1. Obtain C_1, F, d_1. 2. Calculate A = \pi (d_1/2)^2. 3. Calculate \sigma_a = (C_1 \times F)/(2 \times A). 4. Output \sigma_a.
